summary: Joel draws a parallel between Lord Palmerston's famous quote about the impossibly complex Schleswig-Holstein question and the state of modern programming. By invoking a 19th-century diplomatic crisis that drove experts mad, he argues that software development has become unnecessarily complicated. The post is essentially a teaser linking to his longer 'Lord Palmerston and Programming' essay.
key_points: Programming complexity has reached a point where even experts struggle to fully grasp it
The historical analogy to an infamously convoluted diplomatic problem suggests the complexity is systemic, not individual
The brevity of the post itself mirrors the bluntness of the complaint: programming is too hard, full stop
key_insight: Programming has accumulated so much accidental complexity that it rivals historically unsolvable problems in its ability to confound even experts.
